Name: LatencyAwareSpectralRouting
Title: Latency-Aware Spectral Routing: Training-Free Precision Allocation via Real-Time Activation Geometry
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden activations can serve as precise, zero-overhead proxies for dynamic precision allocation, enabling real-time, latency-aware mixed-precision inference without retraining.
Related Work: Existing work in mixed-precision inference often relies on static profiling or costly calibration, which fails to adapt to dynamic input distributions. Recent works like Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) and LightPrune have explored training-free approaches but do not fully address the need for real-time, latency-aware optimization. Our proposal extends these ideas by incorporating dynamic spectral diagnostics and precision-switching policies to enforce strict latency budgets.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference can significantly reduce latency and memory usage but often requires static profiling or costly calibration, which fails to adapt to dynamic input distributions. We propose Latency-Aware Spectral Routing (LASR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry. LASR computes the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states on-the-fly using randomized SVD, mapping these diagnostics to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. Theoretical grounding in PAC-Bayes bounds links spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise, ensuring robust performance under natural distribution shifts. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, LASR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiments: Evaluate the performance of LASR on single-GPU setups using WILDS benchmarks, comparing throughput and accuracy against static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Conduct ablation studies to isolate the impact of spectral diagnostics and precision-switching policies on latency and accuracy.
Measure the computational overhead of real-time spectral diagnostics using randomized SVD and compare it to the overall latency improvement.
Evaluate the robustness of LASR under controlled distributional shifts and adversarial attacks, quantifying the stability of the precision-switching policy.

Perform full cross-layer covariance estimation in offline ablation studies to validate the effectiveness of the spectral diagnostics in capturing the majority of the signal required for precision allocation.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The real-time computation of spectral diagnostics using randomized SVD may introduce additional latency, which needs to be carefully managed to stay within strict latency budgets.
The precision-switching policy may not always perfectly match the optimal precision allocation, leading to suboptimal performance in some cases.
The method's performance may vary across different model architectures and datasets, requiring extensive validation to ensure generalizability.
The theoretical bounds linking spectral conditioning to PAC-Bayes generalization may not fully capture all practical aspects of real-world inference scenarios.
